pages.title: 1. Topological invariance of the flat topos

blocks.html: 1. Topological invariance of the flat topos

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.1

Let \(f : X' \to X\) be a surjective integer morphism radicial. Then the base change functor \[ f^* : X_{\text{ét}} \to X'_{\text{ét}} \] is an equivalence of sites (i.e. an equivalence of category, which is continues as well as any quasi-inverse functor). Consequently the functors \[ f_* : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X'}_{\text{ét}} \] \[ f^* : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X'}_{\text{ét}} \] are equivalences of categories, almost inverses of each other.

The first assertion is well known (SGA IX 4.10 and 4.11) when \(f\) is of finite presentation or \(f\) is flat; the general case reduces to that where \(f\) is of finite presentation. In fact, we already know that the functor \(f^*\) is fully faithful (SGA 1 IX 3.4). It is enough to prove that \(f^*\) is essentially surjective, i.e. that all \(Z'\) spreads over \(X'\) "comes" from a \(Z\) spread over \(X\). Using the fact that \(f\) is a universal homeomorphism, and the full fidelity of \(f^*\), we are brought back to case where \(X\), \(X'\), \(Z'\) are affine, ring spectra \(A\), \(A'\), \(B'\). Writing \(A'\) as the inductive limit of its sub-\(A\)-algebras of finite type \(A'_i\), \(B'\) comes from a spread algebra \(B'_i\) on a \(A'_i\) (cf. EGA IV 8), which gives us brings back to the case where \(A'\) is integer and of type finite on \(A\), therefore finite on \(A\). We then have an \(A\) isomorphism \(A' \simeq A''/J\), or \(A'' \simeq A[t_1, \ldots t_n]\) and \(J\) is an ideal of \(A''\). Writing \(J\) as the inductive limit of its subideals \(J_i\) of finite type, and setting \(A'_i = A''/J_i\), we will still have \(A' = \varinjlim_i A'_i\), therefore \(B'\) comes from an equal algebra \(B'_i\) on a \(A'_i\) (loco quote). On the other hand, as \(\operatorname{Spec} A' \to \operatorname{Spec} A\) is surjective, it is similarly \(\operatorname{Spec} A'_i \to \operatorname{Spec} A\), and moreover we easily verify that since \(\operatorname{Spec} A' \to \operatorname{Spec} A\) is radicial, it is the same for \(\operatorname{Spec} A'_i \to \operatorname{Spec} A\) for \(i\) large enough (apply EGA IV 1.9.9 to \(\operatorname{Spec} A\)(∗)). This brings us back in case \(A'\) is one of \(A'_i\), therefore of finite presentation on \(A\).
This proves the first assertion of 2.1. The fact that \(f_*\) and \(f^*\) are quasi-inverse equivalences of each other as a result immediately.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.2

Let \(F\) be an abelian beam on \(X\), \(F'\) its image inverse on \(X'\), then the canonical homomorphisms \[ (*) \qquad H^i(X,F) \to H^i(X',F') \] are isomorphisms. Similarly, if \(F'\) is an abelian sheaf on \(X'\), \(F\) its image on \(X\), canonical homomorphisms \((*)\) are isomorphisms.
Examples 1.3
Here are common application examples of 1.1:
a) \(X'\) is a subschema of \(X\) having same underlying set that \(X\), i.e. defined by a nil-Ideal \(I\).
b) \(X\) is a schema on a separably closed field \(k\), \(k'\) is an algebraic closure of \(k\), and \(X' = X \otimes_k k'\).

c) Let \(X\) be a geometrically unibranched diagram (for example an algebraic curve on a body \(k\), having as singularities only "cuspidal" singularities, excluding in particular points ordinary doubles). Then, if \(X'\) is the normalized of \(X_{\text{red}}\), by definition \(X' \to X\) is radical, so 2.1 applies.
Remarks 1.4

A morphism \(f\) as in 1.1 is a homeomorphism universal, i.e. is a homeomorphism and remains such by any extension of the base. Conversely, if \(f\) is a universal homeomorphism we prove that \(f\) satisfies the hypotheses of 2.1 (∗), which explains the title of this issue.

Let us expressly point out, regarding example 2.3 c), that if \(X\) is an algebraic curve (over an algebraically closed field \(k\) for fix ideas) having at least one singular point which is not "unibranched" (for example an ordinary double point), then (\(X'\) denoting the normalized of \(X_{\text{red}}\)) the conclusion of 2.2 is already faulty for the \(H^1\) and constant coefficients (compare SGA 1 I 11 a) and SGA 1 IX 5).

footnotes.html: (∗) Let \(S_i \subset \operatorname{Spec} A\) be the set of \(s \in S\) such that the fiber of \(X_i = \operatorname{Spec} A'_i\) in \(s\) is of separable rank 1. Then, the \(S_i\) are constructible parts of \(S\) (EGA IV 9.7.9) forming a growing family, their union is \(S\) according to the hypothesis on \(S' = \operatorname{Spec} A' \to S\) and the fact that the fibers of \(S'_i\) are Noetherian schemes. We therefore have \(S = S_i\) for \(i\) quite large, by EGA IV 1.9.9, cqfd.
(∗) Cf. EGA IV 8.11.6 if \(f\) is of finite presentation, and EGA IV 18.12.11 in the general case.

pages.title: 2. Beams on the spectrum of a body

blocks.html: 2. Beams on the spectrum of a body

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.1

Let \(k\) be a body, \(\bar{k}\) be a separable fence from \(k\), \(\pi = \operatorname{Gal}(\bar{k}/k)\) its topological Galois group, \(X = \operatorname{Spec} k\), \(\overline{X} = \operatorname{Spec} \bar{k}\) (on which \(\pi\) operates on the left). Consider the canonical functor \[ i : X_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \] (defined by \(i(X')(X'') = \operatorname{Hom}_X(X',X'')\)), and the canonical functor \[ j : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \mathscr{B}_\pi \] of the category of beams on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) in the category \(\mathscr{B}_\pi\) (see IV 2.7) (discrete) sets on which \(\pi\) operates continuously on the left, defined by \[ j(F) = \varinjlim_\alpha F(\operatorname{Spec} k_\alpha) , \] where \(k_\alpha\) iterates over the finite subextensions of \(\bar{k}\). Then \(i\) and \(j\) are category equivalences. (As a result, the topos \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) is equivalent (IV 3.4) to the “classifying topos” \(\mathscr{B}_\pi\).)

The compound functor \[ ji: X_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to (\pi\text{-Ens}) \] is an equivalence of categories, according to the fundamental theorem of Galois theory (in the form of SGA 1 V). As \(\mathscr{B}_\pi\) is obviously a topos (II 4.14), it is therefore the same for \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\). Moreover, one family \((X_i \to X)\) of morphisms in \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is covering if and only if it is surjective, i.e. its image in \(\mathscr{B}_\pi\) is surjective, or what amounts to the same thing, covering for the canonical topology of \(\mathscr{B}_\pi\) which shows that the topology of \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is indeed its canonical topology. As a result \(i\) is an equivalence, and it is therefore the same for \(j\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2

The functor \(j\) of 2.1 induces an equivalence of the category of abelian sheaves on \(X = \operatorname{Spec} k\), and the category of \(\pi\)-Galois modules.
Indeed, the latter are precisely the “abelian groups” from the topos \(\mathscr{B}_\pi\). We see the same, by “structure transport”:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Let \(F\) be an abelian beam on \(X = \operatorname{Spec} k\), \(M = j(F)\) the \(\pi\)-associated Galois module, then we have a canonical isomorphism of \(\partial\)-functors in \(F\): \[ H^*(X,F) \simeq H^*(\pi,M) , \] (where the second member designates Galois cohomology, studied by example in Serre C.G.'s course).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

Assume \(k\) separably closed. Then the functor \[ \Gamma : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \] is a category equivalence. If \(F\) is an abelian sheaf on \(X = \operatorname{Spec} k\), we have \(H^i(X,F) = 0\) for \(i \neq 0\). (In other words, the topos \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) is a "punctual topos" (IV 2.2).)

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5

Let \(k'\) be a separably closed extension of a body separably closed \(k\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec} k\), \(X' = \operatorname{Spec} k'\), \(u : X' \to X\) the morphism canonical. Then the functor \[ u^* : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X'}_{\text{ét}} \] is an equivalence of categories.

pages.title: 3. Fiber functors relating to the geometric points of a diagram

blocks.html: 3. Fiber functors relating to the geometric points of a diagram

Let \(X\) be a schema. We will call geometric point of \(X\) any \(X\) schema \(\xi\) which is the spectrum of a body \(\Omega\) separably closed. The data of such a point is therefore equivalent to that of an ordinary point \(x \in X\), and a separably closed extension \(\Omega\) of the residual body \(k(x)\) of \(X\). The most common case for us will be where \(\Omega\) is a separable fence of \(k(x)\), which we will then generally denote by \(\overline{k(x)}\), the corresponding geometric point of \(X\) being then denoted \(\bar{x}\). For \(x \in X\) given, the \(\overline{k(x)}\) (resp. \(\bar{x}\)) are determined to be \(k\)-isomorphism (resp. \(X\)-isomorphism) not unique near.
Remarks 3.2

In most geometric questions, it is more natural to limit oneself to the case \(\Omega\) algebraically closed. The different convention used here is special to the study of sparse topology. This is the convention that we adopted in the definition of the group fundamental SGA 1 V 7. But it should be noted that the locomotive developments citato are also valid with the convention adopted here (because the property of \(\Omega\) which is used there is that any coating spreads spectrum \(\Omega\) is trivial, i.e. precisely that \(\Omega\) is separably closed).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.3

Let \(X\) be a diagram, \(\xi\) be a geometric point of \(X\), \(u : \xi \to X\) its structural morphism. We call fiber functor (geometric) relative to \(\xi\), and we note \(F \mapsto F_\xi\), the functor \[ \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \] composed of \[ \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \xrightarrow{\ u^*\ } \widetilde{\xi}_{\text{ét}} \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma_\xi\ } (\mathrm{Ens}) . \]

We can also say that, thanks to 2.4, a geometric point \(\xi\) of \(X\) defines a point of the topos \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) (IV 6.1), of which we simply take the associated fiber functor (loc.cit).

As the functor \(\Gamma_\xi\) (sections functor on \(\xi\)) is an equivalence categories (12.4), knowledge of fiber functors \(F \mapsto F_\xi\) is essentially equivalent to that of reciprocal image functors \(u^*\). Note also that it immediately follows from 2.5 that if \(\xi\) is a geometric point of \(X\) such that there exists a \(X\)-morphism \(\xi' \to \xi\) (i.e. \(\xi'\) corresponds to a separably closed extension \(\Omega'\) of \(\Omega\)) then the canonical homomorphism \(F_\xi \to F_{\xi'}\) is an isomorphism functional \[ F_\xi \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F_{\xi'} . \] This shows that for the study of fiber functors, we can (even if it means replace \(\Omega\) by the separable algebraic closure of \(k\) in \(\Omega\)) bound in case \(\Omega = k(\xi)\) is a separable fence \(\overline{k(x)}\) from \(k(x)\), and that the fiber functors corresponding to the geometric points of \(X\) located in the same \(x \in X\) are isomorphic with each other (not in a way unique).
We will designate, in accordance with the conventions of 3.1, by \(F_{\bar{x}}\) a fiber functor corresponding to the choice of a separable fence \(\overline{k(x)}\) from \(k(x)\).

Let us point out an obvious transitivity property (which shows the technical usefulness of not being limited exclusively to geometric points defined by separable fences of residual bodies): Let \(f = X' \to X\) be a schema morphism, \(u' : \xi' \to X'\) be a geometric point of \(X'\), then \(u = f u' : \xi' \to X\) is a geometric point of \(X\) which we will note \(\xi\); relative to these geometric points we have a functorial isomorphism in \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\, \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\): \[ f^*(F)_{\xi'} \simeq F_\xi . \] This in fact results from the transitivity formula for image functors inverses \[ (fu)^* \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } u^* f^* . \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.5

Let \(X\) be a schema.

a) For any geometric point \(\xi\) of \(X\), the fiber functor \(F \mapsto F_\xi : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) switches to any \(\varinjlim\) (indexed by categories \(\in \mathcal{U}\)) and finite \(\varprojlim\) (∗). In particular, it transforms bundles in groups (resp. abelian bundles, etc.) in groups (resp. abelian groups, etc...).

b) When \(x\) traverses the points of \(X\), the fiber functors \(F_{\bar{x}}\) form a "conservative" family of functors, i.e. if \(u : F \to G\) is a homomorphism of sheaves on \(X\), \(u\) is an isomorphism if and only if for all \(x \in X\), the corresponding homomorphism \(u_{\bar{x}} : F_{\bar{x}} \to G_{\bar{x}}\) is (∗∗).
Let us note right away, taking into account the properties of correctness of a), the following formal consequences of b):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.6

Let \(u : F \to G\) be a homomorphism of sheaves. Then \(u\) is a monomorphism (resp. an epimorphism) if and only if for all \(x \in X\), \(u_{\bar{x}} : F_{\bar{x}} \to G_{\bar{x}}\) is injective (resp. surjective).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7

Let \(u, v : F \to G\) be two morphisms of sheaves on \(X\), then \(u = v\) if and only if for all \(x \in X\), we have \(u_{\bar{x}} = v_{\bar{x}} : F_{\bar{x}} \to G_{\bar{x}}\). In particular, if \(u,v\) are two sections of \(F\), we have \(u = v\) if and only if we have \(u_{\bar{x}} = v_{\bar{x}}\) in \(F_{\bar{x}}\) for all \(x \in X\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.8

Let \(F \to G \to H\) be a sequence of two homomorphisms of beams on \(X\), then the sequence is exact if and only if for all \(x \in X\), the corresponding sequence \(F_{\bar{x}} \to G_{\bar{x}} \to H_{\bar{x}}\) is.
We will leave it to the reader to deduce the corollaries(∗), which will be moreover partially proven in the demonstration which follows.

Assertion a) results from the accuracy properties already pointed out in (VII 1.4) for any reciprocal image functor, such \(u^* : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \widetilde{\xi}_{\text{ét}}\), and the fact that \(\widetilde{\xi}_{\text{ét}} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) is an equivalence. To prove b), note the following formula, which gives a method for calculating functors fibers:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.9(∗∗)

Let \(\xi \xrightarrow{\ u\ } X\) be a geometric point of \(X\), and let \(C_\xi\) the category of "\(X\)-slack \(\xi\)-punctuated patterns", i.e. couples \((X', u')\), where \(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\) is a schema spread over \(X\), and \(u'\) a \(X\)-morphism \(\xi \to X'\). Then the opposite category \(C_\xi^o\) is filtering and for a beam \(F\) (resp. pre-beam \(P\)) variable on \(X\), we have a canonical functorial isomorphism \[ F_\xi \simeq \varinjlim_{C_\xi^o} F(X'), \qquad (\text{resp. } (aP)_\xi \simeq \varinjlim_{C_\xi^o} P(X') ), \] (where \(aP = \) beam associated with \(P\)).

Indeed, let us first note that for any pre-beam \(P\) on \(\xi_{\text{ét}}\), if \(aP\) is the associated bundle, the natural homomorphism \[ P(\xi) \to aP(\xi) \] is an isomorphism, as it follows for example immediately from the calculation explicit of \(aP\) as \(L(LP)\) (II 3.19.). Let \(\varphi : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{\xi}_{\text{ét}}\) be "reciprocal image" functor, then \(u^* = \varphi^*\) is nothing else (III 2.3.(d)) that the compound \(a\, \varphi'\, i\), where \(i : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widehat{X}_{\text{ét}}\) is the inclusion functor, and \(a : \widehat{\xi}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{\xi}_{\text{ét}}\) the "associated bundle" functor. By the remark previous we find \[ u^*(F)(\xi) = (\varphi'i(F))(\xi) , \] so that it suffices to apply the explicit expression of \(\varphi'\) given in the proof of. The case resp., where we start with a prebundle \(P\) on \(X\), is proved in the same way, using isomorphism functorial \(\varphi^*a(P) \simeq a \varphi(P)\) . The fact that the category \(C_\xi\) is filtering (which remains valid for all \(X\)-schema \(\xi\), not necessarily reduced to a geometric point) immediately results from the fact that in \(C_\xi\) finite projective limits exist, which follows from the fact that \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is a subcategory of \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) stable by projective limits finished.

Let now be \(u : F \to G\) a homomorphism of sheaves on \(X\), such that for all \(x \in X\), \(u_{\bar{x}} : F_{\bar{x}} \to G_{\bar{x}}\) is a monomorphism, We prove that \(u\) is a monomorphism. For this, we must prove that if \(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\), \(\varphi, \psi \in F(X')\) are such that \(u(\varphi) = u(\psi)\), we have \(\varphi = \psi\). Replacing \(X\) with \(X'\) and using the transitivity of functors fibers, we can assume \(X = X'\). For all \(x \in X\), we will have \(u(\varphi)_{\bar{x}} = u(\psi)_{\bar{x}}\) i.e. \(u_{\bar{x}}(\varphi_{\bar{x}}) = u_{\bar{x}}(\psi_{\bar{x}})\), therefore \(\varphi_{\bar{x}} = \psi_{\bar{x}}\) since \(u_{\bar{x}}\) is a monomorphism. Using 3.9 it follows that there exists \(X'_x \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\), whose image contains \(x\), such that \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) have the same image inverse to \(X'_x\). As for \(x\) variable in \(X\), the \(X'_x\) form a covering family of \(X\), it follows that \(\varphi = \psi\).

This proves that if the \(u_{\bar{x}}\) are monomorphisms, it is likewise of \(u\). Let us further suppose that the \(u_{\bar{x}}\) are epimorphisms, let us prove that it is the same for \(u\), therefore that \(u\) is an isomorphism. It remains to be proven that for all \(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\), and all \(\psi \in G(X')\), it exists \(\varphi \in F(X')\) such that \(u(X')(\varphi) = \psi\). We can still assume \(X'=X\), and using 3.9 we see that for all \(x \in X\), exists \(X'_x \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\) such that the image of \(X'_x\) in \(X\) contains \(x\), and a \(\varphi_x \in F(X'_x)\) of which the image in \(G(X'_x)\) is the inverse image of \(\psi\). Using the fact that \(u\) is a monomorphism, we see that the \(\varphi_x\) coincide on the \(X'_x \times_X X'_y\) (\(x,y \in X\)) therefore come from a well-determined \(\varphi \in F(X)\), and we will then have \(u(\varphi) = \psi\) since the inverse images on the \(X'_x\) coincident. c q f d.

blocks.title: Scholia
blocks.html:
Scholia 3.10

Theorem 3.5 implies that the collection of "functors fibers" for the slack topology enjoys the same essential properties than in the usual sheaf theory on a topological space. We will use 3.5 and its corollaries very frequently, and for this reason we will generally dispense with referring to it explicitly.

For all \(x \in X\) we will also designate, by abuse of language, by \(x\) the \(X\)-schema \(\operatorname{Spec} k(x)\), for the usual structural morphism \[ i_x : x = \operatorname{Spec} k(x) \to X . \] It gives rise to a canonical functor \[ i_x^* : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{x}_{\text{ét}} . \] If \(F\) is a beam on \(X\), we will put \(F_x = i_x^*(F)\), so it is a beam on \(x = \operatorname{Spec} k(x)\) (and as such can also be identified with a schema spreads over \(x\), under 2.1). It depends functionally on \(F\), and the functor \(i_x^* : F \mapsto F_x\) still commutes to arbitrary \(\varinjlim\), and \(\varprojlim\) finished, under VII 1.4.

If \(\bar{x} = \operatorname{Spec} \overline{k(x)}\), the fiber functor \(F \mapsto F_{\bar{x}}\) is canonically isomorphic to the compound of the functor \(F \mapsto F_x\) and the functor \(j\) of 1.1 (taking into account that the latter is isomorphic to the fiber functor relating to the geometric point \(\operatorname{Spec} \bar{k}\) of \(\operatorname{Spec} k\)). It immediately follows from this and 3.5 that the system of functors \((F \mapsto F_x)\) (\(x \in X\)) is still conservative. Moreover, if \(k(x)\) is separably closed, i.e. \(x = \bar{x}\), the notations \(F_x\) and \(F_{\bar{x}}\) are strictly contradictory (the first denoting an object of \(\widetilde{x}_{\text{ét}}\), the second a set), but by virtue of 1.1 this is an inessential contradiction.

We see from the preceding remarks that if \(\bar{x} = \operatorname{Spec} \overline{k(x)}\), then for any beam \(F\) on \(X\), the group \(\pi_x = \operatorname{Gal}(\overline{k(x)}/k(x))\) operates in a natural way (on the left) on the fiber assembly \(F_{\bar{x}}\), so that \(F \mapsto F_{\bar{x}}\) can actually be considered a functor \[ \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \mathscr{B}_{\pi_x} \] whose knowledge is essentially equivalent (thanks to 1.1) to that of functor \(F \mapsto F_x\).

This shows, in an important particular case, that unlike to what takes place in the case of sheaves on topological spaces, “fiber functors” generally admit automorphisms non-trivial. In general, we will determine in 7.9 all the homomorphisms of one fiber functor into another.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 3.13

Here is a sometimes useful generalization of 3.5 b). Let's consider a part \(E\) of \(X\) such that for any stale morphism \(f : X' \to X\), \(E' = f^{-1}(E)\) is "very dense" in \(X'\) (EGA IV 10.1.3), i.e. that for any \(f\) as above, any open \(U\) of \(X'\) which contains \(E'\) is equal to \(X'\). Then the fiber functors \(F_{\bar{x}}\) on \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\), for \(x \in E\), already form a "conservative family" (and therefore the corollaries 3.6, 3.7 and 3.8 remain valid by simply choosing \(x \in E\)). This is immediately seen by repeating the demonstration given of 3.5 b). The previous result applies in particular in following situations:

a) \(X\) is a Jacobson diagram (EGA IV 10.4.1.), for example locally of finite type on a field, or on \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbf{Z})\), and \(E\) is the set closed points of \(X\).

b) \(X\) is locally of finite type on a schema \(S\), and \(E\) is the set of points of \(X\) which are closed in their fiber.

c) \(X\) is locally Noetherian, and \(E\) is the set of \(x \in X\) such that \(\bar{x}\) is a finite set (i.e. a semi-local schema of dimension \(\leqslant 1\)) cf. EGA IV 10.5.3 and 10.5.5.

footnotes.html: (∗) It is therefore indeed a “fiber functor” in the sense of Exp. IV.
(∗∗) There are therefore "sufficient fiber functors" of the form \(F \mapsto F_{\bar{x}}\), in the terminology of IV 6.5.

blocks.html: 4. Strictly local rings and schemes
Recall that we say, with Azumaya, that a local ring \(A\) is Henselian if it satisfies the following equivalent conditions:

(i) Any finite algebra \(B\) on \(A\) is direct composite \(\prod_i B_i\) of local rings (the \(B_i\) are then necessarily isomorphic to the \(B_{\mathfrak{m}_i}\), where \(\mathfrak{m}_i\) iterates through the maximal ideals of \(B\)).

(ii) Any algebra \(B\) over \(A\) which is localized to an algebra of finite type \(C\) over \(A\) to a prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) above the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{M}\) of \(A\), and such that \(B/\mathfrak{M}B\) is finite on \(k = A/\mathfrak{M}\), is finite on \(A\).
(iii) Like (ii). but assuming \(C\) spreads over \(A\) in \(\mathfrak{p}\) and \(k \to B/\mathfrak{M}B\) an isomorphism.

(iv) "Hensel's lemma" in classical form is valid for \(A\), i.e. for any unit polynomial \(F \in A[T]\), denoting by \(\dot{F} \in k[T]\) the corresponding reduced polynomial, and for any decomposition \[ \dot{F} = \dot{F}_1 \dot{F}_2 \] of \(\dot{F}\) as a product of two foreign unit polynomials, \(\dot{F}_1\) and \(\dot{F}_2\) are fall (necessarily uniquely) into \(F_1, F_2 \in A[T]\), such that \(F = F_1 F_2\).

For the demonstration of these equivalences and properties generals of Henselian rings, cf. EGA IV 18.5.11. Recall that if \(A\) is any local ring, we show (Nagata) that we can find a local homomorphism, \[ A \to A^h \] with \(A^h\) Henselian, which is universal for local homomorphisms of \(A\) in local Noetherian rings. The ring \(A^h\) is called the henselized from \(A\). The construction given in EGA IV 18.6 (different of that of Nagata) consists of posing \[ A^h = \varinjlim_i A_i , \] where \(A_i\) traverses \(A\)-algebras essentially of finite type and essentially spread over \(A\) (i.e. localized of an algebra spread over \(A\)) such that \(A \to A_i\) is a local homomorphism and the residual extension \(k(A_i)/k(A)\) is trivial. This construction shows in particular that \(A^h\) is flat on \(A\) and if \(A\) is Noetherian, the same is true of its Henselian fence.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.2

A local ring \(A\) is said to be strictly local if it satisfies one of the following equivalent conditions:

(i) \(A\) is Henselian (4.1) and its residual body is separably closed.

(ii) Any local homomorphism \(A \to B\), where \(B\) is located of an algebra spread over \(A\), is an isomorphism.

A schema is said to be strictly local schema if it is isomorphic to the spectrum of a strictly local ring.

The equivalence of (i) and (ii) immediately follows from 4.1. Note that, in geometric form, 4.2 (ii) (resp. 4.1 (iii)) is expressed by saying that for any schema spreads \(X\) over \(Y = \operatorname{Spec} A\), and any point \(x\) of the fiber \(X_y\) (respectively any point of \(X_y\) rational on \(k(y)\)), where \(y\) is the closed point of \(Y\), there exists a section of \(X\) on \(Y\) passing through \(y\) (section which is also necessarily unique).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.3

Let \(X\) be a schema, \(\mathcal{O}_{X_{\text{ét}}}\) the beam on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) defined by \[ \mathcal{O}_{X_{\text{ét}}}(X') = \Gamma(X', \mathcal{O}_{X'}) \] (compare VII 2. c) ), \(u : \xi = \operatorname{Spec}(\Omega) \to X\) a geometric point of \(X\). We call strictly local ring from \(X\) to \(\xi\), and we note \(\mathcal{O}_{X,\xi}\), or simply \(\mathcal{O}_\xi\), the fiber of the beam \(\mathcal{O}_{X_{\text{ét}}}\) at the point \(\xi\). Its specter is called strict localized from \(X\) to \(\xi\).

Under 3.9, we have the description of the fiber \(\mathcal{O}_{X,\xi}\): \[ (*) \qquad \mathcal{O}_{X,\xi} = \varinjlim \Gamma(X', \mathcal{O}_{X'}) , \] where \(X'\) traverses the opposite filter category of the category of schemas spread over \(X\) which are \(\xi\)-punctuated, i.e. provided with a \(X\)-morphism \(\xi \to X'\). According to the transitivity of inductive limits, we can replace in the second member \(\Gamma(X', \mathcal{O}_{X'})\) by \(\mathcal{O}_{X',x'}\), or \(x'\) is the image of \(\xi\) in \(X'\), and we find the expression \[ (**) \qquad \mathcal{O}_{X,\xi} = \varinjlim_i A_i \] where \(A_i\) traverses algebras essentially of finite type and spreads over \(A = \mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) with a \(k\)-homomorphism \(k(A_i) \to \Omega\). (N.B. \(x\) designates the image of \(\xi\) in \(X\)). Of course the transition homomorphisms between the \(A_i\) are the local homomorphisms of \(A\)-algebras \(A_i \to A_j\) which make the corresponding triangle commutative \[ \xymatrix{ k(A_i) \ar[r] \ar[dr] & k(A_j) \ar[d] \\ & \Omega } \] which implies that if there exists an admissible homomorphism of \(A_i\) in \(A_j\), the latter is unique. So the inductive limit \((**)\) is a limit relating to an increasing filtering ordered set of indices.

The description \((**)\) shows that the strict local ring of \(X\) in \(\xi\) essentially only depends on the usual local ring \(A = \mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) (\(x=u(\xi)\)), and the extension \(\Omega\) of \(k = k(A)\). It is also called henselized ring strict of \(A\) (relative to the considered separably closed extension \(\Omega\) of \(k\)) and we will note it \(A^{hs}\). We refer to EGA IV 18.8 for the properties general terms of \(A^{hs}\). Let us simply point out that the given construction shows although \(A^{hs}\) is flat on \(A\), and it is Noetherian if \(A\) is. On the other hand, it is easily shown (loc. cit.) that the local homomorphism \[ A \to A^{hs} \] with \(k\)-homomorphism \[ k(A^{hs}) \to \Omega \] is solution of the universal problem, relating to the data of \(A\) and of the extension \(\Omega\) of \(k\), corresponding to the search for homomorphisms premises \[ A \to A' , \] with \(A'\) strictly local, equipped with a \(k\)-homomorphism \[ k(A') \to \Omega . \] In particular, \(A^{hs}\) is indeed a strictly local ring (which justifies terminology 4.3).

Let's keep the notations of 4.3 and choose a neighborhood open affine \(U\) of \(x\). Note that in 3.9 we can obviously limit ourselves to to \(X'\) which are affine above \(U\). These latter then form a pseudo-filtering projective system of affine schemes, so that we we are in the general situation of VII 5 (cf. VII 5.12 b). Under the expression \((*)\) for \(\mathcal{O}_{X,\xi}\), we will have a \(X\)-isomorphism \[ (***) \qquad \varprojlim_{\substack{X'\ \text{étale} \\ \text{affine sur } U, \\ X'\ \xi\text{-ponctué}}} X' \simeq \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{X,\xi} \] which clarifies the intuitive geometric meaning of \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal{O}_{X,\xi})\) as "limit of slack neighborhoods of \(\xi\)".

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.6

Let \(X\) be a strictly local schema, \(x\) its point closed, which is therefore a geometric point of \(X\). Then the functor \(F \mapsto \Gamma(X,F) : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) is canonically isomorphic to the functor fiber \(F \mapsto F_x\), and subsequently switches to any \(\varinjlim\) (∗).

Indeed, by virtue of def. 4.2 (ii), the final object \(X\) of the category of \(X'\) spreads \(x\)-punctuated on \(X\) considered in 3.9 majore all other objects, so \(\varinjlim F(X')\) is canonically isomorphic to \(F(X)\), cqfd.
In particular, the functor induced by \(\Gamma\) on the category of abelian fascicles is correct, so we conclude

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7

Under the conditions of 4.6 we have for any beam abelian \(F\) on \(X\): \[ H^q(X,F) = 0 \quad \text{pour } q \neq 0 . \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.8

Let \(X\) be a diagram, \(\xi\) be a geometric point of \(X\), \(\overline{X} = \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) the corresponding strict localized scheme, \(F\) a beam variable on \(X\), \(\overline{F}\) its inverse image on \(\overline{X}\), then we have an isomorphism functional \[ F_\xi \simeq \Gamma(\overline{X},\overline{F}) . \]
Indeed, \(\xi\) can be considered as a geometric point of \(\overline{X}\) also, and just apply 4.6 and fiber transitivity (3.4).

pages.title: 5. Application to the calculation of fibers of \(R^q f_*\)

blocks.html: 5. Application to the calculation of fibers of \(R^q f_*\)

Let \[ f : X \to Y \] a morphism of schemas, \(F\) an abelian sheaf on \(X\), we propose to determine the \(R^q f_*(F)\). Under 3.5, it amounts to the same thing, almost, to know the geometric fibers \(R^q f_*(F)_{\bar{y}}\), to \(y \in Y\). Or (V 5.1) \(R^q f_*(F)\) is the beam associated with the prebeam \[ \mathscr{H}^q : Y' \mapsto H^q(X \times_Y Y', F) \] on \(Y\), and subsequently (3.9) \[ R^q f_*(F)_{\bar{y}} = \varinjlim_{Y'} \mathscr{H}^q(Y'), \] where the inductive limit is taken according to the opposite filtering category of the category of \(Y'\) spread over \(Y\) punctuated by \(\bar{y}\). Choosing one affine open neighborhood \(U\) of \(y\), we can limit ourselves within the limit of second member to the cofinal category of \(Y'\) which are affine and above by \(U\). We thus obtain \[ (5.1.1) \qquad R^q f_*(F)_{\bar{y}} \simeq \varinjlim H^q(X \times_Y Y', F), \] where \(Y'\) varies in the previous category. Let's introduce \[ \overline{Y} = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal{O}_{Y,\bar{y}}) = \varprojlim Y' \] (see 4.5), and \[ \overline{X} = X \times_Y \overline{Y} . \]

Note that in the projective system of \(X \times_Y Y'\) (deduced from that of \(Y'\) by base change \(X \to Y\)) the transition morphisms are affine, we are therefore in the general conditions of VII 5.1, and we immediately see, by construction of \(\varprojlim\) in loc.cit., that we have an isomorphism canonical \[ \overline{X} = \varprojlim X \times_Y Y' . \] Let us denote by \(\overline{F}\) the beam on \(\overline{X}\) reciprocal image of \(F\), then we obtains a canonical homomorphism \[ \varinjlim_{Y'} H^q(X \times_Y Y', F) \to H^q(\overline{X},\overline{F}) \] hence, comparing with 5.1.1, a canonical homomorphism \[ (5.1.2) \qquad R^q f_*(F)_{\bar{y}} \to H^q(\overline{X},\overline{F}), \] obviously functional in \(F\).

Now suppose \(f : X \to Y\) quasi-compact and quasi-separated, then it is the same for \(X' \times_Y Y' \to Y'\), and as \(Y'\) is affine, the \(X \times_Y Y'\) are quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Using (5.1.1) and the theorem passing to the limit VII 5.8, we then obtain:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.2

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a quasi-compact and quasi-separate morphism diagrams, \(F\) an abelian beam on \(X\), \(y\) a point of \(Y\), \(\bar{y}\) the geometric point above \(y\), relating to a fence separable \(k(\bar{y})\) from \(k(y)\), \(\overline{Y} = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal{O}_{Y,\bar{y}})\) the corresponding strict localized schema, \(\overline{X} = X \times_Y \overline{Y}\), \(\overline{F}\) the inverse image of \(F\) on \(\overline{X}\). So homomorphism canonical (5.1.2) is an isomorphism: \[ R^q f_*(F)_{\bar{y}} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(\overline{X},\overline{F}) . \]

This statement practically reduces the determination of the fibers of a beam \(R^q f_*(F)\) to the determination of the cohomology of a preschema at above a strictly local diagram, and will be constantly used by the continued. Technically, it is 5.2 which explains the important role of Henselian rings and strictly local rings in the study of flat cohomology.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.3

The statement remains valid for \(R^0 f_*(F) = f_*(F)\), for a any bundle of sets. On the other hand, 5.2 also remains valid for \(R^1 f_*(F)\), when \(F\) is a group beam (not necessarily commutative), taking the usual definition of \(R^1 f_*(F)\) (cf. Giraud’s thesis).

Now suppose that \(f\) is a finite morphism. So \(\bar{f} : \overline{X} \to \overline{Y}\) is a finite morphism, and since \(\overline{Y}\) is strictly local, it follows that \(\overline{X}\) is a finite sum of strictly local schemes \(\overline{X}_i\). Subsequently, using 4.7 we find \[ (5.4.1) \qquad H^q(\overline{X},\overline{F}) = 0 \quad \text{pour } q \neq 0 . \] On the other hand, note that the components \(\overline{X}_i\) of \(\overline{X}\) correspond to the points \(\bar{x}_i\) of \(\overline{X}\) above the closed point \(\bar{y}\) of \(\overline{Y}\), i.e. at the points of \(\overline{X}_{\bar{y}} = X_y \otimes_{k(y)} k(\bar{y})\). Moreover, these points can be considered as geometric points of \(X\), and \(\overline{X}_i\) is then none other than the strict localized schema from \(X\) to \(\bar{x}_i\). We therefore have (4.8) \[ H^0(\overline{X}_i,\overline{F}) \simeq F_{\bar{x}_i} , \] hence \[ (5.4.2) \qquad H^0(\overline{X},\overline{F}) = \prod_i F_{\bar{x}_i} , \] which is a functorial isomorphism into the sheaf of sets \(F\) (useless here to restrict ourselves to abelian fascicles). Taking into account 5.2 and 5.3, the previous formulas (5.4.1) and (5.4.2) give:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.5

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a finite morphism of preschemas, \(y\) a point of \(Y\). Then for any sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), we have an isomorphism (functional in \(F\)) \[ f_*(F)_{\bar{y}} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \prod_{x \in X_y \otimes_{k(y)} k(\bar{y})} F_{\bar{x}} , \] (as a result the formation of \(f_*(F)\) commutes at any change of base \(Y' \to Y\)), and if \(F\) is an abelian sheaf, we have \[ R^q f_*(F) = 0 \quad \text{si } q > 0 . \]

Note that the first formula 5.5 is in fact independent of 5.2 and the general limit passage theorem VII 5.7, and that it implies (thanks to 3.5) that \(F \mapsto f_*(F)\) is an exact functor on the category of abelian bundles, hence again \(R^q f_*(F) = 0\) for \(q \neq 0\). Here's a slight variation of 5.5:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.6

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an integer morphism. So for everything abelian beam \(F\) on \(X\), we have \[ R^q f_*(F) = 0 \quad \text{pour } q \neq 0 . \] Moreover, the functor \(f_*\) on the set sheaves commutes at all base change \(Y' \to Y\).

Indeed, thanks to 5.2 we are brought back to prove that when \(Y\) is strictly local, we have \(H^q(X,F) = 0\) for any abelian beam on \(X\). Now we will have \(Y = \operatorname{Spec} A\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec} B\), \(B\) being an integer algebra, and writing \(B = \varinjlim B_i\), where \(B_i\) traverses the finite type subalgebras of \(B\), (which are even finite on \(A\)), we will have \(X = \varprojlim_i X_i\), where \(X_i = \operatorname{Spec} B_i\). By virtue of VII 5.13, we are reduced to proving that \(R^q f_*(F_i) = 0\) for \(q \neq 0\), \(F_i\) an abelian beam on \(X_i\), which results from 5.5. The last assertion of 5.6 is proven by the same method of reduction to 5.5.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.7

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an immersion morphism. So for any bundle \(F\) on \(X\), the canonical morphism \[ f^* f_*(F) \to F \] is an isomorphism.

As \(f\) factors into the product of an open immersion and of a closed immersion, and that 5.7 is trivial in the case of an immersion open (IV n° 3), we are brought back to the case where \(f\) is an immersion closed. We are brought back to prove that for all \(x \in X\), the homomorphism corresponding \[ (f^* f_*(F))_{\bar{x}} \to F_{\bar{x}} \] is bijective, or by transitivity of the fibers (3.4) the first member is none other than the fiber \(f_*(F)_{\bar{x}}\), so it is necessary to check that the homomorphism canonical \[ f_*(F)_{\bar{x}} \to F_{\bar{x}} \] is bijective, which immediately follows from 5.5.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.8

Using 5.5 and still proceeding as in 5.6, we easily proves that if \(f : X \to Y\) is an integer morphism, \(F\) a bundle in groups on \(X\) (not necessarily commutative), then \(R^1 f_*(F)\) is the final beam on \(Y\) (compare note 5.3).
6. Brackets

Let \(U\) be a Zariski open of the scheme \(X\). So \(U \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\), and in fact \(U\) is a sub-object of the final object \(X\) of \(X_{\text{ét}}\), so defines a sub-object \(\widetilde{U}\) of the final object of \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\), i.e. an "open" of the topos spreads \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) of \(X\) (IV 8.3).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.1

The previous application \(U \mapsto \widetilde{U}\) is an isomorphism of the ordered set of open (in the Zariski sense) of \(X\), on the set of the open topos \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\).

As \(X_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) is fully faithful, we immediately see that the application \(U \mapsto \widetilde{U}\) preserves the order structures i.e. \((U \subset V) \Leftrightarrow (\widetilde{U} \subset \widetilde{V})\), in particular the previous application is injective. To prove that she is surjective, consider a sub-beam \(F\) of the final beam \(\widetilde{X}\), and consider the objects of \(X_{\text{ét}/F}\), i.e. the spread schemas \(X'\) on \(X\) such as \(F(X') \neq \varnothing\). As \(X' \to X\) is slack, it is an application open (EGA IV 2.4.6), in particular its image (in the ensemble sense) \(\operatorname{Im}(X')\) is open. Let \(U\) be the open meeting of \(\operatorname{Im}(X')\) (\(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}/F}\)). As the family of \(X' \to U\) (\(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}/F}\)) is surjective, therefore covering, we conclude that \(U \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}/F}\), therefore \(X_{\text{ét}/F} = X_{\text{ét}/U}\), therefore \(F = U\), cqfd.
Given 6.1, we can therefore speak unambiguously of a "open" of \(X\), without specifying whether we understand this notion in the sense usual Zariski or in the sense of slack topology.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.2

Let \(U\) be an open of \(X\), \(j : U \to X\) the canonical immersion, then the functor \[ j^* : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{U}_{\text{ét}} \] induces an equivalence of categories \[ \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}/\widetilde{U}} \to \widetilde{U}_{\text{ét}} . \]

In fact we immediately check that for any site \(C\) where the \(\varprojlim\) finite objects exist, and for any subobject \(U\) of the final object \(e\) of \(C\), considering the functor \(j : C \to C_{/U}\) defined by \(j(S) = S \times U\), \(j\) is a morphism of sites and \(j^* : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}_{/U}\) induces an equivalence \(\widetilde{C}_{/U} \to \widetilde{C}_{/U}\). It is then sufficient to combine this general fact and the fact that \(U_{\text{ét}}\) is canonically isomorphic to \(X_{\text{ét}/U}\).

Picturesquely, we can express 6.2 by saying that the operations to "induce oneself on an open", in the usual sense of diagrams on the one hand, and in the sense of the topos on the other, are compatible. Here is a similar compatibility for "restriction" operations to a closed":

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.3

Let \(X\) be a schema, \(Y\) be a closed subschema of \(X\), \(U = X - Y\) provided with the induced structure, \(i : Y \to X\) and \(j : U \to X\) canonical immersions. Then the functor \[ i_* : \widetilde{Y}_{\text{ét}} \to \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \] is fully faithful, and if \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\, \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\), \(F\) is isomorphic to a beam of the form \(i_*(G)\) sss \(j^*(F)\) is isomorphic to the final sheaf \(\widetilde{U}\) on \(U\).

Demonstration. Since \(i_*\) and \(i^*\) are deputies of each other, the fact that \(i_*\) is fully faithful is also equivalent to the fact that the homomorphism functional \[ i^*(i_*(G)) \to G \] is an isomorphism, which is none other than 5.7. On the other hand, if \(G \in \mathrm{Ob}\, \widetilde{Y}_{\text{ét}}\), then we check trivially using 6.2 that \(j^*(i_*(G))\) is the final beam on \(U\). Conversely, if \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\, \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) is such that \(j^*(F)\) is the final sheaf, we prove that \(F\) is isomorphic to a beam of the form \(i_*(G)\), or what amounts to the same thing, that canonical homomorphism \[ G \to i_* i^*G \] is an isomorphism. Now it suffices to verify again that for all \(x \in X\), the homomorphism induced on the geometric fibers in \(\bar{x}\) is an isomorphism. When \(x \in U\), this is none other than the hypothesis made on \(G\). When \(x \in Y\), by transitivity of the fibers we are brought back to verify that the homomorphism on the fibers in \(\bar{x}\) induced by \[ i^*(G) \to i^*(i_*(i^*(G))) \] is an isomorphism, but the previous homomorphism is an isomorphism from 5.7 applied to \(F = i^*G\) and \(i\). This completes the demonstration of 6.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.4

The functor \(i_*\) induces an equivalence of the category abelian sheaves on \(Y\) with the category of abelian sheaves on \(X\) whose restriction to \(U = X - Y\) is zero.
In accordance with common usage we will therefore often identify an abelian beam on \(Y\) to an abelian beam on \(X\) zero on \(U\).

By virtue of 6.1 we therefore know (if \(X\) is a schema) interpret the "opens" of the topos \(E = \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) as open \(U\) of \(X\) at usual meaning, and by virtue of 6.3 with this identification the "topos residual" \(E_{\complement U}\) of IV 3 is canonically equivalent to the topos \(\widetilde{Y}_{\text{ét}}\), where \(Y = X - U\) is provided with any induced structure, making \(Y\) a diagram. We can therefore apply to this situation the results of IV 3., in particular the IV 3.3 description of the beams \(F\) on \(X\) in terms of the triples \((F', F'', u)\) where \(F'\) is a beam on \(Y\), \(F''\) a sheaf on \(U\) and \(u\) a homomorphism from \(F'\) into \(i^*j_*(F'')\). We will subsequently freely use the notations \(i^!\), \(j_!\) of IV.3, which designate functors \[ j_! : (\widetilde{U}_{\text{ét}})_{ab} \to (\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}})_{ab} , \] \[ i^! : (\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}})_{ab} \to (\widetilde{Y}_{\text{ét}})_{ab} , \] where the subscript "ab" denotes the category of abelian bundles. These functors give rise to the two exact sequences IV 3.7.

Taking into account the above developments and the terminology general introduced in IV 8.5, it is necessary to introduce, for an abelian beam \(F\) on \(X\), or a section \(\varphi\) of such a beam, the notion of support of \(F\) resp. of \(\varphi\), as being the closed one (in the sense usual, i.e. Zariski) complementary to the largest open on which the object in question cancels itself.

In the current situation, it is also necessary to replace general notations V 4.3 \(H^q(E_{\complement U}, F)\), \(H^q_{\complement U}(F)\) by notations \(H^q_Y(X,F)\), \(\underline{H}^q_Y(F)\), the first designating an abelian group, the second an abelian beam on \(Y\) (or an abelian beam on \(X\), null on \(U\)). Thus, we have \(\underline{H}^q_Y = R^q i^!\) etc...
I refer to V 6 for the general properties of functors previous ones.
7. Specialization morphisms of fiber functors

We saw in No. 4 how we associate, at any geometric point \(\xi\) of schema \(X\), a strictly local \(X\)-schema \[ \overline{X}(\xi) = \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{X,\xi} , \] depending, in fact, only on the geometric point above the image \(x\) of \(\xi\) defined by the separable fence \(\overline{k(x)}\) of \(k(x)\) in \(\Omega = k(\xi)\). We will often restrict ourselves later to geometric points \(\xi = \operatorname{Spec} \Omega\) separable algebraics on \(X\), i.e. such that \(\Omega\) is a separable fence from \(k(x)\), i.e. \(\xi = \bar{x}\). Let's call a \(X\)-schema \(Z\) a strict localized of \(X\) if it is \(X\)-isomorphic to a schema of the form \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{X,\bar{x}}\), we therefore see that \[ \xi \mapsto \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{X,\xi} = \overline{X}(\xi) \] is an equivalence of the category of geometric points algebraic separable on \(X\), with the category of \(X\)-schemas which are strict localities of \(X\), when we take as morphisms in one and the other category only isomorphisms.
This last statement is no longer exact when we take as morphisms all \(X\)-morphisms, because in the second category there can be have \(X\)-morphisms that are not isomorphisms. We then pose the

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.2

Let \(\xi\), \(\xi'\) be two geometric points of the diagram \(X\), we call specialization arrow from \(\xi'\) to \(\xi\) all \(X\)-morphism between the corresponding strict localities \[ \overline{X}(\xi') \to \overline{X}(\xi) . \]
We say that \(\xi\) is a specialization of \(\xi'\) or that \(\xi'\) is a generalization from \(\xi\), if there is a specialization arrow from \(\xi'\) to \(\xi\).

Note that the specialization arrows consist of obvious way, so that by taking as morphisms the arrows of specialization, the geometric points of \(X\) form a category, equivalent (as well as the full subcategory formed by geometric points algebraic and separable on \(X\)) to the subcategory full of \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\) formed from the strict localizations of \(X\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3

Let \(X\) be a schema, \(Z\) be a \(X\)-schema which is isomorphic to a pseudo-filtering projective limit of \(X\)-sequential schemes \(X_i\), with affine transition morphisms (VII 5.1), \(\xi'\) a point geometric of \(X\), \(Z' = \overline{X}(\xi')\) the corresponding strict localized. So:

a) The restriction application \[ \operatorname{Hom}_X(Z',Z) \to \operatorname{Hom}_X(\xi',Z) \] is bijective.

b) For both members to be non-empty, it is necessary and sufficient that the image \(x'\) of \(\xi'\) in \(X\) or in that of \(Z\).

c) Let \(T\) be a \(Z\)-schema, so that \(T\) is a strict localization of \(Z\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be a strict localization of \(X\).

Demonstration. a) we are immediately brought back to the case where \(Z\) is one of \(X_i\), i.e. where \(Z\) is spread over \(X\), and by the change of base \(Z' \to X\) we can assume that \(Z' \simeq X\), hence the conclusion by 4.2 (ii). b) We note that if \(x'\) is the image of a point \(z\) of \(Z\), then \(k(z)\) is necessarily a separable algebraic extension of \(k(x')\), so there exists a \(k(x')\)-homomorphism of the latter in \(k(\xi')\), hence the conclusion.
c) The demonstration is an easy exercise left to the reader.
We conclude from 7.2 and 7.3:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.4

Let \(\xi\), \(\xi'\) be two geometric points of the diagram \(X\). Then the application of restriction defines a bijection of the set \(\operatorname{Hom}_X(\overline{X}(\xi'), \overline{X}(\xi))\) of the specialization arrows of \(\xi'\) in \(\xi\), with the set of \(X\)-morphisms of \(\xi'\) in \(\overline{X}(\xi)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.5

For \(\xi\) to be a specialization of \(\xi'\), it is necessary and it suffices that it is the same for the images \(x, x'\) of \(\xi\), \(\xi'\) in \(X\), i.e. that \(x\) belongs to the adherence of \(\{x'\}\).

As \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{X,\xi} \to \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is faithfully flat (4.4) it is surjective, and it is therefore sufficient to apply the second assertion from 7.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.6

For any schema \(X\), let \(\operatorname{Pt}(X)\) be the category of geometric points on \(X\) (or algebraic geometric points separable on \(X\)), the morphisms being the specialization arrows. Let \(\xi\) be a geometric point of a diagram \(X\), \(\overline{X}(\xi)\) located strict corresponding, we then have an equivalence of the categories \[ \operatorname{Pt}(\overline{X}(\xi)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \operatorname{Pt}(X)_{/\xi} , \] obtained by associating, with any geometric point \(\xi'\) of \(\overline{X}(\xi)\), the point corresponding geometry on \(X\), with the specialization morphism in \(\xi\) deduced from the structural morphism \(\xi' \to \overline{X}(\xi)\) thanks to 7.4.

In other words, the data of a generalization \(\xi'\) of the point geometric \(\xi\) is essentially equivalent to the data of a geometric point from \(\overline{X}(\xi)\). Note also that by virtue of 7.3 c), the homomorphism corresponding \(\overline{X}(\xi') \to \overline{X}(\xi)\) makes \(\overline{X}(\xi')\) the strict localization of \(\overline{X}(\xi)\), relative to \(\xi'\).

Let us now interpret, for any geometric point \(\xi\) of \(X\) and any beam \(F\) on \(X\), the fiber \(F_\xi\) as being \(\Gamma(\overline{X}(\xi), \overline{F}(\xi))\), where \(\overline{F}(\xi)\) is the inverse image of \(F\) on \(\overline{X}(\xi)\) (4.8). So we see that any specialization arrow \[ u : \xi' \to \xi \] induces a homomorphism, functorial in \(F\): \[ u^* : F_\xi \to F_{\xi'} , \] called specialization homomorphism associated with the specialization arrow \(u\). It is evident from the transitivity of the inverse images of beams, which we have for a specialization arrow composed of: \[ (wu)^* = u^* w^* . \]

If \(E\) is a topos, recall (IV) that we called "functor fiber", or (by abuse of language) "point" of the topos \(E\), any morphism of the "final topos" (Ens) (isomorphic to the category of sheaves on a space reduced to a point!) in \(E\), i.e. any functor \[ \varphi = E \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \] which switches to lim. any inductive, and to lim. finite projectives. It is appropriate to consider the set of fiber functors of \(E\) as the set of objects of a full subcategory of \(\operatorname{Hom}(E,(\mathrm{Ens}))\), called category of fiber functors of the topos \(E\), the opposite of which is called category of points of \(E\), and noted \(\operatorname{Pt}(E)\), cf. (IV 6.1).

When \(E\) is of the form \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\), where \(X\) is a schema, we defined in 3.3 and 7.7 a functor \[ (*) \qquad \operatorname{Pt}(X) \to \operatorname{Pt}(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}) , \] where the first member is defined in 7.6. This established, we have the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.9

Let \(X\) be a schema. The previous functor \((*)\) is a equivalence of the category of geometric points on \(X\) (with as morphisms the specialization arrows) with the category of points of the spread topos \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\) (opposite of that of the fiber functors on \(\widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\)).
As this theorem will no longer be used in the rest of the seminar, we limit ourselves here to a demonstration sketch, where we allow certain liberties with universe questions.

a) The functor considered is fully faithful. For all \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\), or \(\check{X} : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) defined by \[ \check{X}(F) = \operatorname{Hom}(X,F) = F(X) . \] Note that the fiber functor \[ \mathscr{E}_\xi : F \mapsto F_\xi \] can be written \[ \mathscr{E}_\xi \simeq \varinjlim_i \check{X}_i , \] where the \(X_i\) are affine schemes spread over \(X\), indexed by a certain filter category (see 4.3 and 4.5). We therefore have for any functor \(\varphi : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\): \[ \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{E}_\xi, \varphi) \simeq \varprojlim_i \operatorname{Hom}(\check{X}_i, \varphi) , \] on the other hand it is well known that we have a bifunctional isomorphism \[ \operatorname{Hom}(\check{X}_i, \varphi) \simeq \varphi(X_i) . \]

When \(\varphi\) is of the form \(\mathscr{E}_{\xi'}\), then \[ \varphi \simeq \varinjlim_j \check{X}_j , \] we therefore have a natural bijection \[ (*) \qquad \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{E}_\xi, \mathscr{E}_{\xi'}) \simeq \varprojlim_i \varinjlim_j \operatorname{Hom}_X(X'_j, X_i) . \] On the other hand we have (in the category of diagrams) \[ \overline{X}(\xi) = \varprojlim_i X_i , \qquad \overline{X}(\xi') = \varprojlim_j X'_j , \] hence \[ \operatorname{Hom}_X(\overline{X}(\xi'), \overline{X}(\xi)) \simeq \varprojlim_i \operatorname{Hom}_X(\overline{X}(\xi'), X_i) , \] on the other hand, as \(X_i\) is locally of finite presentation on \(X\), we has \[ \operatorname{Hom}_X(\overline{X}(\xi'), X_i) \simeq \varinjlim_j \operatorname{Hom}_X(X'_j, X_i) , \] hence \[ (**) \qquad \operatorname{Hom}_X(\overline{X}(\xi'), \overline{X}(\xi)) \simeq \varprojlim_i \varinjlim_j \operatorname{Hom}_X(X'_j, X_i) . \] Comparing \((*)\) and \((**)\) gives the desired conclusion (subject to a compatibility check, left to the reader).
b) The functor considered is essentially surjective.

Let us first notice that if \(C\) is a site where the finite \(\varprojlim\) are representable, \(\widetilde{C} = E\) the corresponding topos, then any fiber functor \(\varphi\) on \(E\) can be represented as a "filtering inductive limit" of functors of the form \[ \check{Y}(F) = F(\widetilde{Y}) = \operatorname{Hom}(\widetilde{Y}, F), \qquad Y \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C , \] (where \(\widetilde{Y}\) is the beam associated with \(Y\)), in the following way (∗). We consider the category \(C_{/\varphi}\) of the couples \((Y, \xi)\), with \(Y \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\), \(\xi \in \varphi(\widetilde{Y})\) (morphisms being defined in the obvious way), and we note that we has an obvious homomorphism \[ (*) \qquad \varinjlim_{(Y,\xi) \in C_{/\varphi}} \check{Y} \to \varphi \] noticing that \[ \operatorname{Hom}(\varinjlim_{C_{/\varphi}} \check{Y}, \varphi) \simeq \varprojlim_{C_{/\varphi}} \operatorname{Hom}(\check{Y}, \varphi) \simeq \varprojlim_{C_{/\varphi}} \varphi(\widetilde{Y}) . \] Now we have a canonical element in \(\varprojlim_{C_{/\varphi}} \varphi(\widetilde{Y})\), by associating with all \((Y, \xi)\) the element \(\xi\) of \(\varphi(\widetilde{Y})\). Using the fact that the finite \(\varprojlim\) exist in \(C\), and the functor \(Y \mapsto \varphi(\widetilde{Y})\) commutes there, we see that in \(C_{/\varphi}\) finite \(\varprojlim\) exists; a fortiori \(C_{/\varphi}\) is filtering. Using that any beam \(F\) is the inductive limit of the beams of the form \(\widetilde{Y}\), and using the fact that \(\varphi\) commutes at inductive limits, we easily conclude that the homomorphism of functors \((*)\) is bijective, and therefore gives the announced representation.

Let us also note that if \(E\) is a topos, \(Y\) an object of \(E\), hence a topos \(E_{/Y}\), then the data of a fiber functor \(\varphi\) for \(E_{/Y}\) is equivalent to the data of a pair \((\varphi, \xi)\), where \(\varphi\) is a fiber functor for \(E\), and \(\xi \in \varphi(Y)\). To the fiber functor \(\psi : E_{/Y} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) we associate the pair \((\varphi, \xi)\), where \(\varphi\) is the compound \(Z \mapsto \varphi(Z) = \psi(Z \times Y)\), and where \(\xi \in \varphi(Y) = \psi(Y \times Y)\) is the image of the unique element of \(\psi(Y)\) by the diagonal morphism of \(Y \times Y\).

Let's now return to the case of the site \(C = X_{\text{ét}}\), and either \(\varphi : \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) a fiber functor on the spread topos of \(X\). Considering the restriction of \(\varphi\) to the full subcategory of \(X_{\text{ét}}\) formed of the openings of this topos, or what amounts to the same (6.1) of open \(U\) of \(X\), we find an application \[ \varphi_o : \mathcal{U}(X) \to \{0, 1\} \] commuting to arbitrary sups and infinites. (We note that for \(U \in \mathcal{U}(X)\), \(\varphi(U)\) is empty or reduced to a point, and we take \(\varphi_o(U) = 0\) or 1 depending on whether we are in one or the other case).

We see easily, thanks to the fact that every closed irreducible of \(X\) has a generic point and a single(∗), that \(\varphi_o\) is defined using a unique \(x \in X\), by the condition \[ \varphi_o(U) = 1 \quad \text{sss} \quad x \in U , \] i.e. \[ \varphi(U) \neq \varnothing \Longleftrightarrow x \in U . \] Let \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}\, \widetilde{X}_{\text{ét}}\), then the image of \(F\) in the final beam \(\widetilde{X}\) is one open; and like \[ \varphi(F) \to \varphi(U) \] is surjective, we see that \(\varphi(F) \neq \varnothing\) sss \(\varphi(U) \neq \varnothing\), i.e. sss \(x \in U\). Let then \(C_{/\varphi}\) be the category of couples \((X', \xi)\) where \(X' \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\), and \(\xi \in \varphi(X')\). It was noted in 7.9.1 that \(C_{/\varphi}\) is a connected filter. Also, thanks to 7.9.2 and the above applied to \(X'\) instead of \(X\), if \((X', \xi) \in \mathrm{Ob}\, X_{\text{ét}}\), it is associated with a single point \(x' \in X'\), such that for an equal morphism \(X'' \to X'\), \(\xi\) is in the image of \(\varphi(X'') \to \varphi(X')\) sss \(x'\) is in that of \(X''\). We conclude from this that the schema \(\varprojlim\) of \(X'\) following the category \(C\) of \((X', \xi)\) exists and is a strict localized \(Z\) of \(X\), therefore corresponding to a geometric point \(\xi\) and a fiber functor \(\mathscr{E}_\xi\).

For any beam \(F\), we then have \[ \mathscr{E}_\xi(F) \simeq \varinjlim_{C_\varphi} F(X') . \] Taking into account 7.9.1 we conclude that \(\varphi\) is isomorphic to \(\mathscr{E}_\xi\), which completes the demonstration of 7.9.

footnotes.html: (∗) This is a special case of IV 6.8.3.
(∗) i.e. \(X\) is sober in the terminology of IV 4.2.1. The assertion made is a special case of IV 4.2.3.

pages.title: 8. Two spectral sequences for integer morphisms

blocks.html: 8. Two spectral sequences for integer morphisms

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.1

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a surjective integer morphism, \(F\) an abelian beam on \(Y\). For any integer \(i\), let \(\mathscr{H}^i(F)\) be pre-beam on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/Y}\) defined by \[ \mathscr{H}^i(F)(Z) = H^i(Z, F_Z) , \] where \(F_Z\) is the inverse image of \(F\) to \(Z\). So there is a sequel spectral (functional in \(F\)) \[ H^*(X,F) \Longleftarrow E_2^{pq} = H^p(X/Y, \mathscr{H}^q(F)) \] ("descent spectral sequence").

Of course, the symbol \(H^p(X/Y, G)\), for a pre-beam \(G\) on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/Y}\), denotes the \(p\).th relative Čech cohomology group, defined using the complex of \(C^n(X/Y,G) = G(X^{n+1})\), where \(X^m\) denotes the power \(m\).th in \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/Y}\). To establish 8.1, let \(p_n : X^{n+1} \to Y\) the projection, and let's put \[ A^n = p_{n*}(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{X^{n+1}}) , \] where \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{X^{n+1}}\) denotes the constant beam \(\mathbf{Z}\) on \(X^{n+1}\). Then the \(A^n\) are the components of a simplicial abelian sheaf on \(Y\), therefore of a abelian bundle complex \(A^*\) on \(Y\). Note that we have a homomorphism obvious \[ (*) \qquad \underline{\mathbf{Z}}_Y \to A^0 . \] This established, we have the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.2

The complex \((A^*)\) with the homomorphism \((*)\) is a resolution from \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_Y\). More generally for any abelian beam \(F\) on \(Y\), \(A^* \otimes_{\mathbf{Z}} F\) is a resolution of \(F\).

Proof: We can assume \(Y\) affine, therefore \(Y = \operatorname{Spec} A\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec} B\), where \(B\) is an \(A\) integer algebra. We will have \(B = \varinjlim_i B_i\), where \(B_i\) traverses the finite subalgebras of \(B\), hence \(X = \varprojlim_i X_i\), where \(X_i = \operatorname{Spec} B_i\). Using VII 5.11, we see that the augmented complex \(A^*(X/Y)\) is then the inductive limit of the complexes increased \(A^*(X_i/Y)\). This brings us back to the case where \(f\) is finished. It is enough to prove that for any geometric point \(\bar{y}\) of \(Y\), the complex \(A^*_{\bar{y}}\) is a resolution of \(\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_{\bar{y}}\), and the rest after tensorization by a \(F\). But if \(X_{\bar{y}}\) is the fiber of \(X\) in \(\bar{y}\), it follows from 5.5 that the complex \(A^*_{\bar{y}}\) is none other than the analogous complex \(A^*(X_{\bar{y}}/\bar{y})\). This gives us brings us back to the case where \(Y\) is the spectrum of a separably closed body \(k\). Using 1.3 a) and b), we can even assume \(k\) algebraically closed, and \(X\) reduces, therefore \(X\) of the form \(I_Y\), where \(I\) is a finite set. But then the complex \(A^* \otimes F\) is identified with the trivial cochain complex of the set of indices \(I\) with coefficient in \(F_{\bar{y}}\), so it is indeed a resolution of \(F_{\bar{y}}\).

We therefore obtain, as a consequence of 8.2, a spectral sequence \[ H^*(Y,F) \Longleftarrow E_2^{pq} = H^p(H^q(Y, A^* \otimes F)) , \] and it remains to clarify the initial term. But we have a homomorphism canonical \[ A^n \otimes F \to p_{n*} p_n^*(F) , \] and the latter is an isomorphism, as we still see by reduction to case \(f\) finished and transition to fibers. Using 5.5, we conclude \[ H^q(Y, A^n \otimes F) \simeq H^q(X^{n+1}, p_n^*(F)) = \mathscr{H}^q(F)(X^{n+1}), \] which gives the initial term announced in 8.1.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 8.3

a) When we give ourselves a recovery locally finite of \(Y\) by closed sets \(Y_i\), and that we put \(X = \coprod_i Y_i\) then the canonical morphism \(f : X \to Y\) is finite, and the spectral sequence 8.1 has an initial term which is explained as the cohomology of complex defined by \(H^q(Y_{i_0 \ldots i_p}, F|Y_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\), where \(Y_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = Y_{i_0} \cap \ldots \cap Y_{i_p}\). This is therefore the analogue of the spectral sequence de Leray for a locally finite closed cover of a topological space ordinary (TF Chapter II 5.2.4). The latter can also be also generalize into a spectral sequence relating to a "finite" morphism i.e. specific to finished fibers, proceeding as in 8.1.

b) Note the analogy of the spectral sequence of 8.1 with the Leray spectral sequence of a covering \((X_i)\) of \(Y\) (in the occurrence by \(X_i\) spread over \(Y\)); the latter would be obtained formally by writing the spectral sequence 8.1 for \(X = \coprod_i X_i\). It is in fact probable that these two spectral sequences admit a common generalization, which would be valid each time we have a family of morphisms \((X_i \to Y)\), which is "effective descent family universal" for the fiber category of beams spread over a variable base diagram (see no. 9 below). The analogous question is poses moreover in ordinary topology, about a common generalization of the two species of Leray spectral sequences of an overlap, supposed either open or closed locally finished (∗).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.4

Let \(Y\) be a schema, \(\pi\) be a profinite group, \((\pi_i)_i\) the projective system of discrete finite quotient groups of \(\pi\), \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) a projective system of main coatings of \(Y\), of groups \(\pi_i\), the transition homomorphisms \(X_j \to X_i\) being compatible with homomorphisms \(\pi_j \to \pi_i\) on groups of operators, \(X = \varprojlim X_i\) (see VII 5.1), so that the group \(\pi\) operates on the \(Y\)-scheme \(X\), \(F\) an abelian beam on \(Y\). So we have a "Hochschild-Serre" spectral sequence (functional in \(F\)) \[ H^*(Y,F) \Longleftarrow E_2^{pq} = H^p(\pi, \varinjlim_i H^q(X_i, F_{X_i})) , \] where \(F_{X_i}\) is the inverse image of \(F\) on \(X_i\), and \(H^p(\pi, -)\) denotes the Galois cohomology.

The term \(E_2^{pq}\) written here is also (by definition of \(H^p(\pi, -)\) and transitivity of inductive limits) isomorphic to \[ E_2^{pq} = \varinjlim_i H^p(\pi_i, H^q(X_i, F_{X_i})) , \] which shows us that it is enough to find an inductive system of sequences spectral (depending on index \(i\)) \[ H^*(Y, F) \Longleftarrow {}^i E_2^{pq} = H^p(\pi_i, H^q(X_i, F_{X_i})) . \] This brings us back to defining the spectral sequence in the case where \(\pi\) is finite. Then \(f : X \to Y\) is a covering morphism in \(Y_{\text{ét}}\), so we can write the Leray spectral sequence of this morphism. Considering isomorphisms canonicals: \[ X^n \simeq X \times_\pi G^n , \] a well-known calculation then shows that for any pre-beam \(\mathscr{H}\) on \(X_{\text{ét}}\), transforming sum into products, \(C^*(X/Y, \mathscr{H})\) is none other than the complex of homogeneous cochains of \(G\) with coefficients in \(\mathscr{H}(X)\), hence the form announced for the initial term.

A variation of this demonstration, avoiding any calculation, consists of considering \(E \mapsto X \times_\pi E\) as a morphism of the site of \(\pi\)-finite sets in the slack site of \(Y\), and to write the continuation Leray spectral of this morphism. Finally, when \(\pi\) is finished, we can also look at the Hochschild-Serre spectral sequence as being a special case of 8.1 relative to the morphism \(f : X \to Y\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.5

Suppose \(Y\) quasi-compact and quasi-separated, then the spectral sequence of 8.4 is written \[ H^*(Y,F) \Longleftarrow E_2^{pq} = H^p(\pi, H^q(X,F_X)) . \]
Indeed, by virtue of VII 5.8, we then have isomorphisms canonical \[ \varinjlim_i H^q(X_i, F_{X_i}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(X, F_X) . \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.6

Let \(Y\) be a local closed point Henselian scheme \(y\), \(F\) an abelian beam on \(Y\), \(F_o\) the induced beam on \(Y_o = \operatorname{Spec}(k(y))\). Then the canonical homomorphisms \[ H^n(Y,F) \to H^n(Y_o, F_o) \] are isomorphisms.

Let \(\bar{y}\) be a geometric point on \(y\), corresponding to a separable fence \(\overline{k(y)} = k(\bar{y})\) from \(k(y)\); as \(Y\) is Henselian, the localized strict \(X\) from \(Y\) to \(\bar{y}\) is the projective limit of the coverings related Galois slacks \(\bar{y}\)-punctuated \(X_i\), so that we are under the conditions of application of 8.5. Like \(H^q(X, F_X) = 0\) for \(q > 0\) under from 4.7, we conclude isomorphisms \[ H^n(Y,F) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } H^n(\pi, F(X)) , \] where \(\pi\) is the "Galois group" of \(X\) on \(Y\), isomorphic to that of \(\overline{k(y)}\) of \(k = k(y)\). We find the same (or by 2.3) \[ H^n(Y_o, F_o) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } H^n(\pi, F_o(X_o)) , \] where \(X_o = X \times_Y Y_o \simeq \operatorname{Spec}(\overline{k(y)})\). But the restriction homomorphism \(F(X) \to F_o(X_o)\) is an isomorphism under 4.8, which immediately results conclusion 8.6.

footnotes.html: (∗) Since writing these lines, P.Deligne has made a general theory of “spectral descent sequences”, cf. Exp. Vbis.

blocks.html: 9. Descent of slack beams
This issue will no longer be used in the remainder of this Seminar, and can be omitted on first reading.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.1

Let \(f : X' \to X\) be a surjective morphism (resp. universally submersible (SGA 1 IX 2.1)) of diagrams. Then the functor \[ f^* : X_{\text{ét}} \to X'_{\text{ét}} \] is faithful and "conservative" (cf. 3.6) (resp. induces a functor fully faithful to the beam category on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) in the beam category beams on \(X'_{\text{ét}}\) provided with descent data relative to \(f : X' \to X\)).

The first point immediately results from the transitivity of the functors fibers (3.4) and (3.7), the second (which is also expressed by saying that \(f\) is a descent morphism relative to the category fibered beams spread over variable diagrams), also means that for two beams \(F, G\) on \(Y\), the natural diagram \[ (*) \qquad \operatorname{Hom}(F, G) \to \operatorname{Hom}(F', G') \rightrightarrows \operatorname{Hom}(F'', G'') \] is exact, where \(F'\) and \(G'\) (resp. \(F''\) and \(G''\)) are the inverse images of \(F\) and \(G\) on \(X'\) (resp. on \(X'' = X' \times_X X'\)). Taking for \(F\) the final bundle, the statement gives the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.2

Let \(f : X' \to X\) be a universally submersive morphism. Then for any beam \(F\) on \(X\), the diagram \[ \Gamma(X, F) \to \Gamma(X', F') \rightrightarrows \Gamma(X'', F'') \] is exact, where \(X'' = X' \times_X X'\), and where \(F', F''\) are the inverse images from \(F\) to \(X'\), \(X''\).
We will prove 9.1 using the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.3

Assume \(f\) universally submersive. Let \(F\) be a beam on \(X\), let us designate by \(S(F)\) the set of sub-beams of \(X\), and similarly define \(S(F')\), \(S(F'')\). So the diagram natural applications \[ S(F) \to S(F') \rightrightarrows S(F'') \] is correct.

Demonstration. The fact that \(S(F) \to S(F')\) is injective results immediately because the functor \(f^*\) is conservative; because if \(F_i\) (\(i = 1, 2\)) are two sub-bundles of \(F\) such that \(f^*(F_1) = f^*(F_2)\), then the inclusions \(F_3 = F_1 \cap F_2 \to F_i\) (\(i = 1, 2\)) are isomorphisms, because they become such after application of the functor \(f^*\), therefore \(F_1 = F_2\). It remains to prove that if \(G'\) is a sub-bundle of \(F'\) such that \(\mathrm{pr}_1^*(G') = \mathrm{pr}_2^*(G')\), then \(G'\) is the inverse image of a sub-bundle of \(F\). Note that we can find an epimorphism \(F_1 \to F\) in \(X_{\text{ét}}\), or \(F_1\) is representable by a sparse diagram on \(X\). Let's introduce \(F_2 = F_1 \times_F F_1\), and likewise \(F'_1\), \(F''_1\), \(F'_2\), \(F''_2\), bundles giving rise to a set diagram \[ \xymatrix{ S(F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & S(F') \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] \ar[d] & S(F'') \ar[d] \\ S(F_1) \ar[r] \ar@<0.5ex>[d] \ar@<-0.5ex>[d] & S(F'_1) \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] \ar@<0.5ex>[d] \ar@<-0.5ex>[d] & S(F''_1) \ar@<0.5ex>[d] \ar@<-0.5ex>[d] \\ S(F_2) \ar[r] & S(F'_2) \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & S(F''_2) } \] in which the columns are exact, thanks to the correctness properties in the beam category on \(X, X', X''\) respectively. A standard diagram-chasing then shows that, to prove that the first line is correct, it is enough to prove it for lines 2 and 3. But for line 2 this results from the fact that \(F_1\) is representable, \(X' \to X\) universally submersive, and SGA IX 2.3. On the other hand, \(F_2\) is also representable, because it is a sub-sheaf of \(F_1 \times_X F_1\) which is representable, and we apply 6.1. So line 3 is also correct, cqfd.

Let us now prove 9.1, i.e. that every morphism \(u' : F' \to G'\), compatible with descent data on \(F', G'\), \(u'\) comes from a morphism \(u : F \to G\). Let \(H = F \times G\), therefore \(H' = F' \times G'\), \(H'' = F'' \times G''\), then the graph of \(u'\) is a subsheaf \(\Gamma'\) of \(H'\), whose two inverse images are equal to the graph of the same morphism \(u'' : F'' \to G''\). So under 9.3, \(\Gamma'\) comes from a sub-bundle \(\Gamma\) from \(H\). I say that \(\Gamma\) is the graph of a morphism \(u : F \to G\), i.e. that the morphism \(p : \Gamma \to F\) induced by \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) is an isomorphism: indeed, it becomes an isomorphism after the base change \(X' \to X\), and we apply the already proven part of 9.1. The morphism \(u : F \to G\) then answers the question, cqfd.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 9.4

Let \(f : X' \to X\) be a surjective morphism of schemas. It is assumed that one of the following hypotheses is true:

a) \(f\) is integer.

b) \(f\) is clean.

c) \(f\) is flat and locally of finite presentation (∗).

d) \(X\) is discrete (e.g. the spectrum of a body).

Then \(f\) is an effective descent morphism for the category fibered \(\mathscr{F}\) on \((\mathrm{Sch})\) of spread beams on diagrams variables, i.e. the functor \(f^*\) induces an equivalence of the category beams on \(X\) with the category of beams on \(X'\), equipped with descent data relative to \(f : X' \to X\).

Let \(F'\) be a beam on \(X'\), provided with descent data relative to \(f : X' \to X\). We then define a functor \[ G : X_{\text{ét}}^o \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \] by the formula \[ G(Y) = \operatorname{Ker}(F'(Y') \rightrightarrows F''(Y'')) . \] We immediately see that \(G\) is a beam for the flat topology, on the other hand we have a canonical injective homomorphism \(G \to f_*(F')\), hence a homomorphism \(f^*(G) \to F'\), and the latter is obviously compatible with descent data. It remains to be examined whether this homomorphism is an isomorphism. Note that \(G\) is also definable by the accuracy of \[ G \to f_*(F') \rightrightarrows g_*(F'') , \] therefore for any change of base \(Y \xrightarrow{\ h\ } X\) which switches to the formation of \(f_*(F')\) and \(g_*(F'')\), \(h^*(G) = G_Y\) identifies with the "descended" beam of \(F'_Y\), by \(Y' \to Y\), and of course the homomorphism \(f_Y^*(G_Y) \to (F_Y)'\) is that deduced from \(f^*(G) \to F'\) by change of base. But let's suppose that the change of base \(f : Y = X' \to X\) commutes to the formation of \(f_*(F')\) and \(g_*(F'')\), and note that like \(Y' = X' \times_X X'\) has a section on \(Y' = X'\), so \(Y' \to Y\) is an effective descent morphism for any fibered category, in particular for \(\mathscr{F}\), it follows that \(f_Y^*(G_Y) \to (F_Y)'\) is an isomorphism, so \(f^*(G) \to F'\) becomes a isomorphism after base change \(Y' = X' \times_X X' \to X'\). Like this last has a section, \(f^*(G) \to F'\) is an isomorphism, so the data descent envisaged on \(F'\) is effective.

This proves Theorem 9.4 in case a), thanks to 5.6. Case b) also follows from the fact that if \(f\) is clean, then \(f_*\) commutes at any base change \(Y' \to Y\), (which will be proven in a subsequent presentation (XII 5.1 (i))).

In case c), the question being local on \(X\) we can assume \(X\) affine, and that there exists an affine schema \(X'_1\), of presentation finished, almost finished and faithfully flat on \(X\), and a \(X\)-morphism \(X'_1 \to X'\), which brings us back to the case where \(f\) is almost finished. Even if it means locating again on \(X\), in the sense of the flat topology this time, we see that it will exist an open \(X'_1\) of \(X'\) such that \(X'_1 \to X\) is finite and surjective, which brings us back to the case where \(f\) is finite and surjective, already treated in a).

In case d), we can assume (by locating on \(X\)) that \(X\) is reduced to a single point, and even given 1.1, it is spectrum of a body \(k\). Additionally, \(f\) is universally open (EGA IV 2.4.9) therefore we are under the conditions of 9.1, and the reasoning of 9.4.1 applies again, taking a change of base with \(Y = \operatorname{Spec} k\), where \(k=k(x)\), for a \(x \in X\). But then it is still true that \(f_*\) switches to change basic \(Y \to X\), as we will see later (XVI 1.4 and 1.5).
This completes the demonstration of 9.4.
